Participant Information Sheet

Study Title: Parental Toy Preferences for Children

Researcher: [Laya Prior], School of Applied Sciences, Abertay University

Supervisor: [Prof Vera Kempe], Abertay University

You are being invited to take part in a research study. Before you decide whether to participate, please read the following information carefully.

What is the purpose of this study?

This study explores how people choose toys for children. You will be shown pairs of toy images and asked which toy you would choose for an imaginary child.

What will I be asked to do?

You will complete two short blocks of toy-choice questions (9 choices each), plus a brief demographics questionnaire. The study should take approximately 5–10 minutes.

Who can take part?

Anyone aged 18 or over can participate.

Are there any risks?

There are no known risks. You may withdraw at any time by closing your browser. If you withdraw, your data will not be saved.

What happens to my data?

Your responses are anonymous and stored securely. No personally identifying information is collected. Data will be used for academic purposes only and may be included in an Honours dissertation and related publications. Data will be retained in accordance with Abertay University data management policy.

Contact

If you have any questions, please contact [2206588@abertay.ac.uk] or [v.kempe@abertay.ac.uk].

Consent

About You

Please answer all questions.

There are 9 choices in this section.

Section 1 complete!

Thank you. Now you will complete a similar set of choices, but this time for a different imaginary child.

Debriefing

Thank you for completing this study!

What was this study about?

This study investigated whether adults show gender-typed toy preferences when choosing toys for imaginary sons versus daughters, and whether the strength of this preference varies by the gender of the adult making the choice.

You were shown pairs of toys from three categories: toys typically marketed to boys (e.g., action figures, toy cars), toys typically marketed to girls (e.g., dolls, tea sets), and gender-neutral toys (e.g., Play-Doh, craft sets). Your pattern of choices across these pairs allows us to measure the degree and direction of any gender-typing bias.

Why does this matter?

Research suggests that adults’ toy choices for children can reinforce gender stereotypes from an early age. Understanding the degree to which prospective parents show these biases — and whether this differs between men and women — can inform discussions about gendered socialisation.

Your data

Your responses are fully anonymous. If you would like to withdraw your data, please close this page now and your responses will not be submitted. By clicking the button below, your anonymous data will be submitted.

If you have any questions about this study, please contact [STUDENT EMAIL] or [SUPERVISOR EMAIL].
